question: Why might a TCP socket receive only part of a message in a single 'data' event?

options.text: TCP is a stream protocol — it doesn't guarantee message boundaries
Node.js splits messages for optimization
options.explanation: Node.js doesn't intentionally split data. TCP is stream-oriented, not packet-oriented. Data may arrive in arbitrary chunks

options.text: This only happens on slow connections
options.explanation: TCP data fragmentation happens regardless of speed. Chunk sizes depend on MSS, OS buffers, and network conditions

options.text: This is a bug in the net module
options.explanation: This is not a bug but a fundamental TCP property. To delimit messages, you need framing (newline-delimited, length-prefix, etc.)

question: What type of stream is IncomingMessage (req) in an HTTP server?

options.text: Readable Stream
Writable Stream
options.explanation: req is a Readable Stream (you read the request body from it). Writable Stream is ServerResponse (res), which you write the response to

options.text: Duplex Stream
options.explanation: req is Readable only. To read the request body, use for await...of or the 'data' event

options.text: Transform Stream
options.explanation: req is Readable — it only provides data for reading. Transform both reads and writes with transformation

question: Why can dns.lookup() become a bottleneck in high-load applications?

options.text: lookup() uses the libuv thread pool, which has only 4 threads by default
lookup() makes requests slower than resolve()
options.explanation: The DNS query speed itself may be similar. The problem is that lookup() blocks a thread from the pool, and there are only 4 by default

options.text: lookup() doesn't cache results
options.explanation: The OS resolver used by lookup() has its own cache. The problem is not caching but blocking the limited thread pool

options.text: lookup() doesn't support IPv6
options.explanation: lookup() supports both IPv4 and IPv6. The problem is thread pool blocking with many concurrent calls

question: Which statements about TLS in Node.js are true?

options.text: The minimum recommended version is TLS 1.2
rejectUnauthorized: false disables certificate verification
socket.getProtocol() returns the TLS version in use
HTTPS runs over UDP, not TCP
options.explanation: HTTPS runs over TCP (via the net module). HTTP/3 uses QUIC (over UDP), but standard HTTPS is TCP + TLS

question: What happens if you don't call res.end() in an HTTP handler?

options.text: The client will wait for the response indefinitely (timeout)
Node.js automatically calls end() after 30 seconds
options.explanation: Node.js doesn't automatically call end(). The server has a default timeout (2 minutes) after which the connection closes, but this is not normal behavior

options.text: The response will be sent with an empty body
options.explanation: Without end(), response headers may be sent (if writeHead was called), but the connection stays open and the client keeps waiting

options.text: The server will crash with an error
options.explanation: The server won't crash — it just leaves the connection open. This leads to resource leaks and client hanging
